title: RAG Overview
text: Retrieval augmented generation (RAG) is a design pattern that combines a retrieval subsystem with a large language model. The retriever supplies relevant source passages to the model, which then conditions answers on factual context from the source documents. RAG pipelines are widely used in search, question answering, customer support, and knowledge management systems.

title: ChromaDB
text: ChromaDB is a vector database built for embeddings. It supports fast similarity search over dense vectors and can persist collections to disk using DuckDB and Parquet. It is ideal for production semantic search systems because it can store metadata alongside embeddings and scale across many documents.

title: Chunking Strategies
text: Chunking strategies decide how text is broken into retrievable pieces. Fixed chunking uses a consistent window size with overlap. Recursive chunking splits by paragraphs and sentences to preserve semantics. Semantic chunking groups text by natural sentence boundaries so that each chunk remains coherent.

title: MMR Reranking
text: Maximum Marginal Relevance (MMR) improves retrieval by balancing relevance with diversity. It selects items that are both similar to the query and dissimilar from already chosen candidates. This helps the search engine avoid returning many overlapping snippets and surfaces broader coverage of the corpus.

title: Embedding Pipeline
text: A robust embedding pipeline uses batching, caching, and stable identifiers. Batching reduces API request overhead. Caching prevents redundant embedding calls on unchanged data. Storing chunk ids, text, and metadata together makes retrieval fast and reproducible.
